caption.text:
An island where entering means vanishing. No rescue. No body recovery. No exceptions.

North Sentinel Island, Andaman & Nicobar. Home to the Sentinelese — the most isolated tribe on Earth, protected by Indian law under the 1956 Andaman and Nicobar Islands Protection of Aboriginal Tribes Regulation. A 5-kilometer exclusion zone. Navy patrols. Strictly illegal to land.

In January 2006, fishermen Sunder Raj (48) and Pandit Tiwari (52) drifted into the shallows after their anchor failed. The Sentinelese killed them with axes. When the Indian Coast Guard helicopter arrived to recover the bodies, the tribe attacked it with arrows and spears. The mission was abandoned. The bodies were reportedly placed on bamboo stakes facing the sea — a warning.

In November 2018, American missionary John Allen Chau paid fishermen to take him to the island. He was shot with arrows, dragged away, and buried. Police opened a murder case against 'unknown tribal members' but made no arrests. The US government confirmed it would not press charges. The body was never recovered.

In March 2025, a Ukrainian-American YouTuber landed illegally, left a can of Diet Coke and a coconut, and was arrested by Indian police. He survived only because he didn't stay.

The Sentinelese have made their policy clear for 60,000 years: trespassers disappear. The Indian government agrees — and protects their right to enforce it. No one who lands there returns unchanged. Most don't return at all.

Is this murder, or is it the last true border on Earth?

text.line_2: Is this murder, or the last true border?
